general_notes: Configuration for foundational experiments research table used by research strategists and lab PIs to ground new hypotheses in replicated experiments across domains (AI, biology, climate, physics). Focus on bibliographic accuracy, experimental details, and reproducibility assessment.

search_groups.description: Core paper/experiment identification and temporal context - used for row identification and search context
Field classification, experimental essence, and datasets/systems - foundational research characteristics typically found together in academic sources
Key experimental results, limitations, and reproducibility status - outcome information typically discussed together in reviews and meta-analyses
Hypothesis links and next experiment design - strategic research planning information requiring synthesis across sources

validation_targets.description: Full citation or experiment name with primary authors - serves as unique identifier for the foundational work
examples: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al.)
validation_targets.notes: Primary identifier - must include paper title or experiment name with key authors in parentheses

validation_targets.description: Publication year or experimental timeframe for the foundational work
validation_targets.notes: Can be single year or range for multi-year experiments. Part of unique identification.

validation_targets.description: Primary research domain or interdisciplinary field classification
validation_targets.notes: Use slash notation for interdisciplinary work. Should reflect primary domain focus.

validation_targets.description: Core experimental approach, methodology, or key innovation in concise technical terms
validation_targets.notes: Technical but accessible description of the fundamental experimental approach or breakthrough

validation_targets.description: Primary experimental system, dataset, or biological/physical system studied
validation_targets.notes: Specific systems, datasets, or experimental contexts. Use slash for multiple related systems.

validation_targets.description: Primary experimental outcome or achievement in concise terms
validation_targets.notes: Focus on the main empirical finding or demonstrated capability

validation_targets.description: Known constraints, challenges, or boundaries of the experimental approach
validation_targets.notes: Concise listing of key limitations, can be comma-separated for multiple constraints

validation_targets.description: Assessment of replication status, verification methods, or reproducibility challenges
validation_targets.notes: Focus on actual replication efforts, verification standards, or reproducibility assessment

validation_targets.description: Specific connection between this foundational work and current working hypothesis
validation_targets.notes: Actionable connection describing how this work informs or constrains the current hypothesis

validation_targets.description: Proposed follow-up experiment or research direction building on this foundation
validation_targets.notes: Specific experimental design or research question that extends this foundational work

clarifying_questions:
I configured this as a research strategy tool focusing on foundational experiments across multiple domains (AI, biology, climate, physics). I set up four search groups: paper identification, research methodology, results assessment, and hypothesis integration. I used sonar-pro for the more complex synthesis tasks (results/hypothesis groups) while keeping sonar for basic fact-checking. 

A few questions to optimize this for your research workflow:

1. I assumed you want comprehensive validation of all experimental details - would you prefer to focus validation efforts on specific columns like reproducibility and hypothesis links while treating others as reference-only?

2. I configured higher-end models (sonar-pro) for results assessment and hypothesis integration since these require synthesis across sources - are you comfortable with the additional cost for better accuracy on these strategic elements?

3. I treated both "Paper/Experiment" and "Year" as ID fields since they jointly identify each foundational work - would a single identifier column work better, or do you need both for your research tracking?

config_change_log.instructions: Generate an optimal configuration for this data validation scenario. User provided these answers to clarifying questions: This 10×10 table serves research strategists and lab PIs who want to ground a new hypothesis in replicated, foundational experiments across domains (AI, biology, climate, physics). Rows list well-known papers/experiments (e.g., Transformer, CRISPR-Cas9) with real bibliographic anchors; columns capture experimental essence, datasets/systems, key results, limitations, reproducibility notes, and a dedicated “Hypothesis Link” describing how the experiment informs a specific working hypothesis. Analysts use it to select precedent work, assess evidence quality, and design next experiments. Hyperplexity scales this by auto-filling citations, extracting protocols, flagging replication issues, and tracking which prior results most strongly constrain the proposed hypothesis.

config_change_log.ai_summary: Set up comprehensive validation for your foundational experiments research table. Created focused search strategies for different types of information: basic paper details, research methodology, experimental results and limitations, and strategic hypothesis connections. Used more advanced AI models for the complex synthesis tasks like assessing reproducibility and linking experiments to your current hypothesis, while keeping efficient models for straightforward fact-checking. All 10 columns will be validated to ensure accuracy across bibliographic details, experimental outcomes, and strategic research planning elements.
config_change_log.technical_ai_summary:
**Table Purpose**: Research strategy tool for grounding new hypotheses in foundational experiments across domains (AI, biology, climate, physics). Used by research strategists and lab PIs to select precedent work, assess evidence quality, and design next experiments.

**Unique Identifiers**: "Paper/Experiment" and "Year" jointly identify each foundational work - both marked as ID importance since they provide essential context for all other validations.

**Search Groups Strategy**:
- Group 0: Paper identification (Paper/Experiment, Year) - provides essential context for all searches
- Group 1: Research methodology (Field, Essence, Dataset/System) - foundational characteristics typically found together in academic sources  
- Group 2: Results assessment (Key Result, Limitations, Reproducibility Notes) - outcome information typically discussed together in reviews and meta-analyses
- Group 3: Hypothesis integration (Hypothesis Link, Next Experiment) - strategic planning requiring synthesis across sources

**Model Selection Rationale**:
- Groups 0-1: sonar for straightforward fact-checking of bibliographic and methodological details
- Groups 2-3: sonar-pro for complex synthesis of results, limitations, and strategic connections requiring deeper reasoning

**Column Classifications**: All 10 columns included as required. Paper/Experiment and Year as ID fields (joint identifier), remaining 8 as CRITICAL since they serve the table's core research strategy purpose.

**Key Assumptions**:
- Research strategists need comprehensive validation across all experimental aspects
- Hypothesis links and next experiments require higher-level synthesis capabilities
- Reproducibility assessment is critical for research quality
- Cross-domain nature requires robust search context (high setting)

**Clarification Urgency (0.5 - MODERATE)**: Configuration covers all requirements but several strategic decisions could benefit from clarification around validation priorities, cost/accuracy tradeoffs for synthesis tasks, and identifier structure preferences.

storage_metadata.description: Configuration for foundational experiments research table used by research strategists and lab PIs to ground new hypotheses in replicated experiments across domains (AI, biology, climate, physics). Focus on bibliographic accuracy, experimental details, and reproducibility assessment.
